identity.title: All Other Amusement and Recreation Industries

lens.included_activities: US operators of fixed-site participatory recreation venues in the roughly $1-10M normalized EBITDA band, including miniature golf and driving ranges, archery and shooting ranges, billiard parlors, escape rooms, go-kart or similar single-attraction venues, and comparable facilities selling repeat admission, reservations, memberships, parties, or events to external customers.
lens.excluded_activities: Outdoor adventure and guide operators, riding stables, recreational day camps, sports teams and leagues without facilities, boating and social clubs, mobile amusement-device and ride concession operators, fireworks services, captive amenities, non-operating property shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Direct consumer and group-event revenue from admissions, timed reservations, activity fees, memberships or passes, parties, food and beverage, and ancillary merchandise; repeat use is common but most revenue is not protected by long-term contracts.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 713990 is unusually heterogeneous, combining fixed-site venues, outdoor guides, day camps, teams and leagues, clubs, riding stables, and mobile concession operators. The lens is narrowed to fixed-site participatory recreation venues because their on-site staffing, reservation/admission workflow, safety obligations, direct pricing, and transferable facility operations form a coherent screen; excluded models have materially different labor, seasonality, contract, asset, and transfer dynamics. The narrowing is for coherence, not attractiveness.

a.bridge: BLS measures the broader NAICS 713000 workforce rather than the narrowed 713990 venue population: amusement and recreation attendants are 13.78% of employment, management occupations 5.37%, and the table also shows substantial food-service and supervisory work. I map booking, ticketing, waivers, customer messaging, event sales, staffing, marketing, purchasing, and routine reporting to AI exposure while excluding direct activity supervision, safety, cleaning, maintenance, food preparation, and most live hospitality. The broader dataset includes casinos, parks, golf, fitness, and bowling, so the direction of error is ambiguous; gambling and large attractions may add standardized transactional work, while fixed-site 713990 venues may have more owner-manager administration hidden in frontline roles.
a.rationale: The venue workflow contains a meaningful digital and administrative layer, but delivery remains physical and guest-facing. Exposure is concentrated in reservations and ticketing, lead handling, waiver processing, scheduling, marketing, customer support, forecasting, and manager paperwork rather than supervision and operation of the attraction itself.
a.change_evidence: A task-and-payroll diary from at least 50 LMM mini-golf, range, escape-room, billiard, and go-kart venues, stratified by subtype and role, would permit a direct wage-weighted estimate.
caveats: No occupation table specific to the narrowed fixed-site 713990 lens was found.
The frozen l=0.3878 is code-wide, uses 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, and is harmonized from a QCEW/SUSB construction; it may not describe the narrowed venue mix.
Owner labor and seasonal staffing may be poorly represented in employer occupation data.

rho.bridge: Agilysys reports deployed hospitality technologies that automate reservations, activity scheduling, self-service ordering and payment, reporting, inventory, loyalty, and AI-enabled checkout, and states that kiosk self-service reduces on-site labor. Lucky Strike separately reports kiosks, robotic process automation, online reservations and event sales, and a leaner staffing model across location-based entertainment. These are adjacent large-customer and large-operator populations, not a measured five-year implementation share for LMM fixed-site 713990 venues. I haircut for legacy systems, integration cost, fragmented vendors, owner-manager capacity, safety exceptions, and service-quality constraints. Both proxies likely bias readiness upward, though packaged cloud tools could lower adoption barriers.
rho.rationale: More than half of the exposed work appears implementable within five years because booking, ticketing, payments, marketing, customer messaging, scheduling, waivers, and reporting already have commercial digital analogues. The remaining gap reflects messy integrations, small-firm change capacity, cybersecurity and payment requirements, unpredictable peak traffic, and the need for human exception and safety handling.
rho.change_evidence: Venue-level implementation cohorts from reservation/POS vendors such as ROLLER, Clubspeed, or equivalent providers, reporting pre/post labor hours, conversion, exceptions, customer ratings, cost, and deployment time by subtype, would materially move rho.
caveats: Vendor claims cover hospitality and leisure broadly and do not isolate AI from conventional workflow software.
Lucky Strike's scale, brand, and centralized systems are not representative of most LMM venues.
Already automated work is outside a by definition and must not be counted as new implementation.

e.rationale: The code explicitly includes many business models excluded by the coherence narrowing—guides, camps, teams, clubs, riding stables, and mobile concessions—as well as the included fixed-site venue types. With no current subtype-by-LMM-firm cross-tabulation, roughly half of the frozen-band firms are judged to fit the venue lens, with a wide range reflecting the residual code's composition.
e.change_evidence: Classify all 288 frozen-band firms by primary activity and operating model using Census Business Register microdata or a licensed business database, websites, permits, and facility addresses; then verify EBITDA-band and control eligibility firm by firm.
caveats: The Census examples establish heterogeneity but provide no subtype shares for the frozen EBITDA band.
The frozen n=288 is a margin-bridged code-wide estimate and may be materially misleading after lens narrowing.
Mixed venues and multi-activity firms may not admit a clean primary-activity classification.

s5.bridge: An SBA-hosted cross-industry survey says 29% of owners expected to exit within five years and 37% wanted a family transfer. Lucky Strike reports 75 location-based entertainment venue acquisitions since fiscal 2022, confirming active strategic buying in adjacent venue categories. I reduce the generic exit intention for family succession, closure, minority recapitalization, landlord asset sales, and businesses too owner-dependent to transfer. The SBA measure likely overstates qualifying transfers, while the public-company count both overstates comparability by including bowling, water parks, and other FECs and understates the private market by observing one buyer.
s5.rationale: Fixed-site recreation venues have identifiable assets, permits, customer traffic, and operating histories that can transfer, and adjacent strategic acquisitions are observable. However, many owner exits will be internal, distressed, property-only, or closures, and the code's small-business transaction market is opaque.
s5.change_evidence: A five-year matched transaction dataset from BizBuySell and specialist venue brokers, supplemented by deed, liquor-license, range-permit, and local business-license control changes, would materially change s5.
caveats: The SBA survey is old, cross-industry, and measures expected exit rather than completed control transfer.
Lucky Strike's acquisitions are not identified by seller size and include venue categories outside the narrowed lens.
Asset sales of attractions or real estate do not necessarily transfer a qualifying operating firm.

q.rationale: Direct admission, reservation, membership, and party pricing creates no automatic contractual pass-through of labor savings. Operators can retain value through steady list prices, higher conversion, targeted upsells, and improved throughput, but local entertainment alternatives, discounting, group-event negotiation, and customer expectations for better service or refreshed attractions share benefits over renewals and repeat visits.
q.change_evidence: Five-year cohorts linking automation deployment to realized prices, discounts, visit volume, event close rates, membership churn, customer ratings, and EBITDA by venue subtype would directly measure retained gross benefit.
caveats: The venue set spans different local competitive intensity and membership dependence.
Adjacent large-operator disclosures may overstate pricing power and cross-sell capability.
This estimate concerns retention after implementation, not demand growth or implementation feasibility.

d5.bridge: BEA's broad real recreation-services quantity index rose from 94.638 in 2021 to 112.879 in 2025, while SFIA reports 250 million Americans participated in at least one sport, fitness, or leisure activity in 2025 and overall participation grew 1.2% year over year. Census's taxable-employer 71399 revenue rose from $10.5 billion in 2018 to $17.988 billion in 2022, but that series is nominal and code-wide. I use the recent real recreation trend and participation growth to support modest expansion, while applying a wide downside range for discretionary spending, local saturation, weather, novelty decay, and subtype churn. The broad BEA/SFIA proxies likely overstate the narrowed lens if at-home or free activities capture growth; nominal Census revenue also biases quantity growth upward through inflation.
d5.rationale: Participation and real recreation-service consumption have grown, supporting modest five-year quantity growth for accessible social and experiential venues. The service basket remains discretionary, locally competitive, and sensitive to consumer budgets; individual concepts can fade even when aggregate recreation participation rises.
d5.change_evidence: An inflation-adjusted same-venue panel of admissions, booked sessions, memberships, repeat visits, and event counts for mini-golf, ranges, escape rooms, billiards, and go-karts through 2026 would materially change d5.
caveats: BEA recreation services include many activities outside NAICS 713990 and outside the narrowed lens.
SFIA participation counts activities rather than paid venue visits or service quantity.
The Census 71399 series is nominal, ends in 2022 on the fetched page, and includes excluded business models.

o.rationale: Demand for the narrowed service basket is demand to use a physical attraction or supervised facility. Software can self-serve booking, admission, waivers, payment, scoring, and routine guidance, but an accountable operator remains necessary for safety, equipment uptime, cleaning, facility access, food service, incidents, and experience quality. A small portion could shift to unattended access or software-mediated self-service without a conventional venue operator.
o.change_evidence: Comparative operating data from unattended or remotely supervised ranges, mini-golf, billiard, escape-room, and simulator concepts—covering staffing, incidents, uptime, repeat visits, and insurance requirements—would test the eliminable operator share.
caveats: Safety and staffing requirements vary sharply across shooting ranges, go-karts, mini-golf, billiards, and escape rooms.
Remote monitoring, smart access, digital waivers, and automated scoring may reduce on-site labor without eliminating accountable operation.
The estimate applies only to the narrowed fixed-site service basket, not excluded guide, camp, team, or concession models.

narrative.executive_view: The residual NAICS code is too heterogeneous for one coherent operating thesis, so the screen focuses on fixed-site participatory recreation venues. These businesses have repeat admissions, parties, memberships, and events plus a sizable labor base, but their automatable work is primarily the transaction and management layer around a physical attraction. The opportunity is operational standardization across venue subtypes, tempered by weak subtype data and potential mismatch in the code-wide frozen inputs.
narrative.ai_impact: AI and conventional automation can handle booking and ticketing, lead response, waiver processing, customer messaging, staff scheduling, promotion creation, purchasing support, forecasting, loyalty offers, and routine performance reporting. Direct attraction supervision, safety intervention, cleaning, maintenance, food preparation, and live exception handling remain human and site-bound. AI is therefore more likely to avoid incremental hiring and widen manager span than to create unattended venues at scale.
narrative.value_capture: Venues set direct admission, reservation, membership, and event prices, so there is little contractual cost pass-through. Savings can be retained through steady pricing, higher conversion, improved throughput, and cross-sell, but local competition, discounting, and customer demand for refreshed attractions will absorb part of the benefit. Visible service degradation would quickly turn labor savings into lower repeat traffic.
narrative.firm_availability: The frozen estimate of 288 LMM firms is code-wide, while only an uncertain share fits the fixed-site venue lens. Adjacent location-based entertainment acquisitions demonstrate strategic appetite, but there is no reliable public transaction census for these subtypes. Family transfers, closures, owner dependence, property-only sales, permits, and mixed-use venues make qualifying firm-level availability substantially lower than generic owner exit intent.
narrative.demand_durability: Broad real recreation-services consumption and sports, fitness, and leisure participation have recently grown, supporting modest aggregate demand growth. Yet these are broad proxies: paid venue visits can lose share to free, outdoor, at-home, or new entertainment formats. Demand is discretionary and concept-specific, so maintaining novelty, safety, reviews, and local relevance is as important as the macro participation trend.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The biggest weakness is classification: the residual code mixes fundamentally different businesses, and neither the frozen l nor n is measured for the narrowed venue population. Other gaps include subtype task weights, independent-center technology adoption, private control-transfer data, and inflation-adjusted same-venue demand. Execution risks include legacy system integration, cyber and payment security, waivers and permits, insurance, seasonal peaks, equipment downtime, local competition, and over-automation of hospitality.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat booking, admission, event-sales, waiver, marketing, scheduling, and reporting workflows can be standardized across a fragmented set of physical recreation venues.
narrative.principal_weakness: The residual code's extreme business-model heterogeneity makes the frozen labor and firm-count inputs—and most public evidence—poor matches for the narrowed operating lens.

sources.title: 2022 NAICS Definition: 713990 All Other Amusement and Recreation Industries
sources.supports: Defines the residual industry and lists fixed-site venues, outdoor adventure operations, day camps, clubs, teams, guides, riding stables, and mobile concession operators; supports the heterogeneity finding, lens narrowing, eligibility uncertainty, and physical operator requirement.

sources.title: May 2023 Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: NAICS 713000
sources.supports: Reports 1,809,830 workers across amusement, gambling, and recreation, including 13.78% amusement and recreation attendants and 5.37% management occupations, with detailed food-service and supervisory roles; supports the broad occupation-mix proxy for a.

sources.title: Agilysys, Inc. Form 10-K for fiscal year ended March 31, 2024
sources.supports: Describes deployed hospitality and leisure tools for booking, activity scheduling, POS, kiosks, mobile ordering and payment, reporting, inventory, loyalty, and AI-powered checkout, and states that self-service reduces on-site labor; supports a, rho, q, and operator-workflow judgments.

sources.title: Lucky Strike Entertainment Corporation Form 10-K for fiscal year ended June 29, 2025
sources.supports: Discloses self-service kiosks, robotic process automation, online reservations and event sales, data-driven offerings, leaner staffing, and 75 location-based entertainment venue acquisitions since fiscal 2022; supports implementation and transfer proxies.

sources.title: Real personal consumption expenditures: Recreation services (chain-type quantity index)
sources.supports: BEA quantity index rose from 94.638 in 2021 to 105.472 in 2022, 109.533 in 2023, 111.069 in 2024, and 112.879 in 2025; supports the broad real-demand proxy for d5.

sources.title: SFIA 2026 Topline Participation Report press release
sources.supports: Reports 250 million Americans participated in at least one of 126 sport, fitness, or leisure activities in 2025, total participation grew 1.2% year over year, and core participation reached 158.8 million and grew 1.3%; supports the participation bridge for d5.

sources.title: 5 Reasons to Hire Your Child
sources.supports: SBA-hosted article cites a survey in which 29% of business owners expected to exit within five years, 54% within ten years, and 37% wanted a family transfer; supports the cross-industry exit-intent proxy for s5.

sources.title: Total Revenue for All Other Amusement and Recreation Industries, Taxable Employer Firms
sources.supports: Census Service Annual Survey series reports nominal taxable-employer revenue of $10.5 billion in 2018, $11.417 billion in 2019, $8.852 billion in 2020, $14.156 billion in 2021, and $17.988 billion in 2022; supports broad code-level demand context with explicit inflation and scope caveats.
